Caption: Simulated bubble dynamics for validation purpose. (a) Radius vs time R(t) curve with varying tissue elasticity modulus G (0, 1, 10, 100 and 1000 kPa). (b) R(t) curve with (indicated by the red dashed lines) and without (indicated by the black solid lines) heat and mass transport at G = 100 kPa. (c) Corresponding bubble temperature profile of (b). The same parameters as employed by [25]: a sinusoidal wave Asin(2πft), A = −1 MPa, f = 1.0 MHz, P 0 = 101.3 kPa, ρ L = 1060 kg m−3, c 0 = 1500 m s−1, λ relax = 3 ns, σ = 0.056 N m−1, μ = 0.015 kg m−1 s−1, R 0 = 1 μm and T 0 = 20 °C, were used in the present calculation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A schematic diagram of the HIFU experimental setup used.
A schematic diagram of the in vivo experimental setup used.

Simulated single bubble dynamics under a 2.0 MHz nonlinear-shocked wave in the liver over 100 acoustic cycles. (a) Nonlinear shocked-waves obtained by numerically solving the KZK equation used to excite a single bubble. (b) Radius vs time curve with varying T 0 (20, 60, 90, 100 °C) at G = 2 kPa. (c) Radius vs time curve with varying shear modulus G (0, 2, 5, 8 and 10 kPa) at T 0 = 100 °C. (d) Molecular contents (water vapour and air) vs time curve at T 0 = 100 °C and 90 °C, G = 2 kPa. (e) Vapour pressure inside the bubble at T 0 = 100 °C and G = 2 kPa. The initial bubble radii R 0 and the tissue relaxation time λ relax were 1 μm and 3 ns [25] respectively.

A comparison of the bubble dynamics in the liver (G = 2 kPa) under 2.0 MHz sinusoidal (P− = −15.6 MPa) and nonlinear-shocked (P+ = 85.4 MPa; P− = –15.6 MPa) excitations at T 0 = 100 °C over 100 acoustic cycles. In the simulation, the initial bubble radius was chosen as R 0 = 40 μm as this clearly demonstrated the effect of the different shapes of the acoustic pressure waveforms on the bubble growth at 100 °C without bubble collapses.

Calculated a time-varying strain produced in the liver caused by rectified bubble growth at 100 °C with varying shear modulus G. (a) Corresponding strain vs time curve of Fig. 4(c) over 100 acoustic cycles. (b) A percentage difference in strain (G = 0, 5, 8, 10 kPa) relative to strain calculated at G = 2 kPa. The ultimate fractional strain values of liver tissue (0.54) and of femoral artery (0.69) ex vivo were obtained from [67].

A sequence of high-speed camera images obtained in an optically transparent liver tissue mimicking gel phantom during the single 10-ms HIFU exposure with P+ of 85.4 MPa and P− of −15.6 MPa. These images were filmed at a 100,000 fps. The 2.0 MHz HIFU beam propagates from left to right. The times at 0 ms (a) and 10 ms (w) correspond to the start and the end of the HIFU exposure respectively. (c), (e) and (g) represent simulated temperature contour plots at t = 1, 2 and 3 ms respectively. Images (x, y, z-i) were captured 0.83, 3.44 and 9.32 ms after the HIFU ceased at 10 ms. (z-ii) Computed peak temperature at the HIFU focus in the tissue phantom.

Acoustic signal emitted from the HIFU focus in the gel phantom during the single 10-ms HIFU pulse. (a) The PCD voltage versus time plot and (b) the corresponding spectrogram. Acoustic emissions were recorded at a sampling frequency of 0.5 GHz. The time at 0 ms represents the start of the HIFU exposure.

A comparison between the experimentally measured bubble radius (mean ± standard deviation SD) and the simulated radius vs time curves. The simulation was performed at the temperature of T 0 = 95.62 °C which was obtained from the BHT simulation at t = 3.47 ms. Physical properties of the tissue phantom were obtained using Eqs. (33)–(37) at T 0 = 95.62 °C. Shear modulus of G = 4.85 kPa of the liver tissue phantom [68] used in the present study was included in the simulation with R 0 = 1 μm. Photron FASTCAM Viewer software (Photron, San Diego, CA, USA) was employed for the size measurement (24 μm/pixel). Each measurement was repeated three times. A movie showing the presence of rectified bubble growth starting from at t = 3.47 ms is available in supplementary 1.
Corresponding computed strain curve of Fig. 9 at G = 4.85 kPa. The ultimate fractional strain of the tissue phantom was assumed to be equal to that of the porcine ex vivo liver tissue of 0.54 [67].

Histological examination around a freshly created cavity produced by an electrical power of 200 W (simulated in situ P+ = 80 MPa, P− = −15.6 MPa) with 1% duty cycle, 1 Hz PRF and 10 HIFU pulses. (a) A photograph showing the cavity creation in vivo. The 2.0 MHz HIFU beam propagates from top to bottom. (b) Corresponding H&E stained lesion. Images (c) to (f) show the highlighted areas in (b) at higher magnifications. Arrows indicate broken hepatocyte plates.
